verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: I praised your holiness on your quitting the envied see.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: Once it was venerable; now it is ridiculous, for we have made it a thing to be bought and sold.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: I was astounded to hear of your having appealed to the men who ejected you.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: You ought to have done just the contrary, and, on being invited to grasp the tiller, to have declined to do so, on the ground that your shipmates had become your foes.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: Are you not aware, most godly sir, what our Saviour, through His sacred apostles, taught us to preach?

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: Do you not know what the heirs of the apostolic doctrines have just now laid down as objects of worship?

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: For who of the old teachers from the time when the message was first preached down to the period of the darkness that now obtains, ever listened to any one preaching one nature of flesh and Godhead or dared at any time to call the nature of the only begotten passible?

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: These doctrines in our day are by some men openly and boldly uttered, while among others their utterance is overlooked, and by silence men become participators in the blasphemy.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: What then, may well be asked, is the proper course to be taken by those who abominate such doctrines?

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: They have, I should reply, two alternatives before them; they may either come to close quarters, and prove the spuriousness of the doctrines, or they may decline communion with their opponents as openly impious.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: I, indeed, have received the wrong done me as a divine blessing.

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: I do not mean that I have thanked them that have wronged me; how could I thank fratricides, and men who have become followers of Cain?

verses.book: Letters of the Blessed Theodoret, Bishop of Cyprus.
verses.text: But I praise my Master for thinking me worthy of the lot of them that suffer wrong, for separating me from wrong-doers and blasphemers, and for giving me my most delightful rest.
